text:
User: Tomorrow I have that doctor visit in the morning. I keep forgetting what I wanted to ask.
Assistant: Let’s make a short list you can bring. What are the top things bothering you lately?
User: The blood pressure numbers, and I get a little foggy if I’m doing too many things.
Assistant: Okay. Here are 5 questions to write down: (1) Are my BP readings controlled and do we need medication changes? (2) Could BP swings cause lightheadedness or brain fog? (3) Any sleep changes I should make? (4) Should I schedule cognitive screening or a referral? (5) What daily routine would help attention and organization?
User: I also lose track in the middle of cooking.
Assistant: Add: “Can you recommend safety steps for cooking and leaving the stove on?”
User: What do I bring?
Assistant: Bring your BP log, medication list, and your questions. Put them by the door tonight.
User: Please remind me to put the notebook and the BP paper together.
Assistant: Put the blue health notebook + BP readings sheet in one folder now, then place it next to your keys.

User: I went to therapy today and they said to use checklists. I feel silly needing that.
Assistant: Checklists aren’t silly—they’re a tool, like reading glasses. What part of the day is hardest?
User: Leaving the house. I start, then I wander and forget what I was doing.
Assistant: Let’s build a 6-step “Leaving Home” checklist: (1) Wallet/ID, (2) Keys, (3) Phone, (4) Appointment card, (5) Meds if needed, (6) Lock door.
User: I forget if I locked the door.
Assistant: Add a confirmation habit: after locking, touch the handle once and say out loud “Door locked.” Then move to step 3.
User: And cooking?
Assistant: Use a timer every time the stove is on. If you step away, the timer comes with you.
User: Can you write it short so I can copy it?
Assistant: Yes: “Keys–Wallet–Phone–Card–Lock–Timer.” Keep it by the entry table.

User: I’m volunteering tomorrow and I’m worried I’ll get mixed up with instructions.
Assistant: We can make it easier. What’s the job there usually?
User: Sorting food donations. Sometimes they talk fast.
Assistant: Try this script: “Hi, I do best with one task at a time—can you show me the next step and where finished items go?”
User: I don’t want to be a problem.
Assistant: That actually helps the team—fewer mistakes. Also, ask who you should check in with if you’re unsure.
User: What if I forget what they just said?
Assistant: Repeat it back: “So I put cans here and bags there, right?” Repeating confirms and helps memory.
User: Okay. I can do that.
Assistant: Great. Tonight, lay out comfortable shoes and your volunteer badge so morning is simpler.

User: I have a bank appointment tomorrow. Bills are confusing lately.
Assistant: Let’s aim for “simple and automatic.” What do you want to happen after tomorrow?
User: I want fewer envelopes and fewer late fees.
Assistant: Good goal. Ask the banker to: (1) confirm all utilities on auto-pay, (2) set up a single “bill pay” account, (3) set low-balance alerts, and (4) turn on paperless statements if you’re comfortable.
User: I’m afraid I’ll miss something if it’s paperless.
Assistant: Then keep paper for the two most important items and go paperless for the rest. Also ask for a monthly printout summary.
User: What should I bring?
Assistant: Photo ID, recent utility bills, and your checkbook if you have one.
User: Can you give me a simple sentence to say?
Assistant: “I’d like to simplify my bills: auto-pay, alerts, and fewer accounts to track.”

User: Tomorrow is the tax help appointment. I can’t remember what papers they want.
Assistant: We’ll make a short “tax folder” checklist. Do you have a letter pile?
User: Yes, a big pile.
Assistant: Pull only these: (1) photo ID, (2) Social Security statement (if you have it), (3) retirement income forms, (4) last year’s return if available, (5) any medical expense summaries, (6) property tax statement if you own a home.
User: I get overwhelmed and start reading everything.
Assistant: Don’t read—just sort by category. Set a 20-minute timer. When it rings, stop.
User: What if I don’t find something?
Assistant: Bring what you have. The preparer can tell you exactly what’s missing.
User: Okay. I’ll do 20 minutes.
Assistant: Great—label an envelope “TAX APPT” and put everything there.

User: Therapy today was about stove safety. I feel embarrassed.
Assistant: Safety planning is responsible, not embarrassing. What’s the main worry—forgetting the stove, or getting distracted?
User: Both. I start soup, then I start a different thing.
Assistant: Let’s do a “one burner rule”: only one burner on at a time. And always set a timer on your phone the moment you turn it on.
User: But I forget the phone too.
Assistant: Keep a kitchen timer on the counter as backup. Also use a bright sticky note: “STOVE ON” placed on the table when cooking starts.
User: I like the note idea.
Assistant: And when you finish: turn off burner, remove note, then say out loud “Stove off.”
User: That would reassure me.
Assistant: If you want, make 3 ‘safe meals’ that are mostly microwave/oven and rotate them on busy days.

User: I’m going to a support group tomorrow for mild cognitive changes. I don’t know what to expect.
Assistant: Usually it’s a small group with a facilitator. People share what’s hard and what helps.
User: I don’t want to scare myself.
Assistant: You can participate as much or as little as you want. It’s okay to just listen.
User: What should I say if they ask why I’m there?
Assistant: Something simple: “I’m noticing attention and organization issues and I’m learning strategies.”
User: They said something about vascular cognitive impairment at my last visit.
Assistant: That often means thinking skills are affected by blood flow/vascular health—so controlling blood pressure, sleep, exercise, and routines can help.
User: So it’s not just ‘forgetting names’?
Assistant: Right—many people notice slower thinking, distraction, trouble planning, and mental fatigue.
User: That sounds like me.
Assistant: Then the group may feel validating, and you might pick up practical tips from others.

User: Tomorrow is the neurology follow-up. My daughter wants me to bring questions, but I go blank in the office.
Assistant: Let’s draft a short list you can hand to the doctor. What are your top concerns day-to-day?
User: I lose track when I’m doing chores. And I feel tired mentally.
Assistant: Questions list: (1) What did my screening show, and what does it mean? (2) Is this consistent with vascular cognitive impairment? (3) What changes help most—BP targets, sleep, exercise? (4) Should we change medications? (5) Any therapy or training you recommend? (6) What safety steps should I follow for cooking/driving?
User: Driving… I’m nervous about it.
Assistant: Add: “How do I know when it’s time to limit driving?”
User: I also forget appointments unless someone tells me twice.
Assistant: Add: “What reminder systems work best—paper calendar, phone alarms, or both?”
User: Can you make it one page?
Assistant: Yes—keep it to 8 bullets. Put it with your appointment card tonight.

User: I did chores today and then I was wiped out. My brain feels slow.
Assistant: That sounds like cognitive fatigue. How long were you working without a break?
User: Maybe an hour? I didn’t notice.
Assistant: Try “pacing”: 15 minutes work + 5 minutes rest. Use a timer so you don’t have to track it mentally.
User: I hate stopping mid-task.
Assistant: Think of the break as part of the task—like charging a battery. It prevents mistakes and frustration.
User: I also start three tasks at once.
Assistant: Use a “one-zone rule”: choose one area (kitchen OR entryway) and finish only that zone before moving on.
User: That’s hard.
Assistant: Put a sticky note on your shirt: “ONE THING.” It sounds funny, but it’s effective.
User: Actually, that might help.
Assistant: Great. For tomorrow’s therapy, tell them: “Timers help me more than trying to remember.”
